title: Adversarial Patches
concept: Adversarial patches are physical-world attacks where a printed sticker or pattern is placed on an object to fool vision systems.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main characteristic of a digital adversarial attack?

options.text: Tiny pixel changes that are invisible to humans but fool AI
Physically breaking the camera lenses with lasers
Removing layers from a model graph

exercises.explain: Digital adversarial attacks use imperceptible math perturbations to trigger high-confidence misclassifications.

cards.title: AI-fooling stickers
cards.body: It's one thing to edit pixels digitally in a computer file. But what if you could fool an AI in the real world? By printing a colorful, custom sticker and putting it on a stop sign, you can make a self-driving car think it's a 45 MPH speed limit sign!

cards.title: What is an Adversarial Patch?
cards.body: An adversarial patch is a physical-world attack. It is a highly salient, printed pattern designed to override a classifier's attention, causing it to ignore the main object and predict whatever class the patch is optimized to represent.

cards.title: A magician's pocket watch
cards.body: Think of a patch like a magician waving a shiny pocket watch. It doesn't matter what else is happening in the room; your eyes (the model's attention) are locked onto the watch, making you blind to the actual trick.

cards.title: The stop sign attack
cards.body: Researchers print a small sticker with a colorful abstract pattern. They place it on a physical stop sign. A YOLO object detector looking at the stop sign misclassifies it as a "speed limit 45" sign with high confidence.

cards.title: Visible but confusing
cards.body: You might think adversarial patches must be invisible like digital noise. In reality, physical patches are highly visible to humans (looking like abstract art or stickers), but their mathematical structure completely scrambles the AI's math.

cards.title: Digital Noise vs. Physical Patch
cards.body: Comparing digital and physical attack surfaces:

left.text:
Modifies entire image slightly
Invisible to humans
Requires access to image file
Fails if photo is printed

right.text:
Modifies localized area heavily
Visible to humans (sticker)
Requires no system access
Succeeds in physical world

exercises.prompt: How do physical adversarial patches differ from digital perturbations?

options.text: They are printed stickers placed in the physical world rather than digital file edits
They are invisible to both humans and computers
They require retraining the model's weights
They are only used to speed up edge inference

exercises.explain: Adversarial patches are physical-world stickers or printouts designed to fool camera systems.

exercises.prompt: True or False: An adversarial patch works by completely dominating the attention maps of a neural network.
exercises.explain: True. The patch contains high-intensity patterns that force the model's feature extractors to ignore other visual cues.

exercises.sentence: A colorful sticker designed to trick an object detector in the real world is an adversarial ___.
exercises.explain: Physical stickers optimized to deceive models are called adversarial patches.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a real-world example of an adversarial patch attack?

options.text: Wearing custom-patterned glasses to fool face recognition cameras
Renaming a JPG file to PNG to crash a server
Deleting validation images from the training set

exercises.explain: Special glasses with optimized patterns are a physical patch attack that disrupts face recognition.

exercises.prompt: You are building a security system for an automated bank vault. A hacker tries to enter by wearing a printed paper badge with an abstract pattern that makes the pedestrian detector classify them as a 'backpack'. What type of attack is this?
exercises.explain: Because the hacker used a printed physical badge, this is a physical adversarial patch attack targeting the model's detection capabilities.
